perturbation.original_text: 2. Definition . "Confidential Information" includes, but is not limited to prototypes, trade secrets, intellectual property, information, technical data, research, products, software , services, development, macros, source code, unreleased software, inventions, ideas, processes, designs, drawings, engineering, marketing , markets , customer information, business plans, business policies or practices, forecasts or financial information, team process, design process, part supply, pricing, development process and procedures , disclosed by Party One and Par t y T w o under this Agreement which at the time of disclosure is designated as confidential (or like designation), is disclosed in circumstances of confidence, or would be understood by the parties, exercising reasonable business judgment , to be confidentia l. Confidential Information shall include, but not be limited to, all materials marked "Confidential Information".
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Definition . "Confidential Information" includes only information explicitly labeled as 'Confidential' in writing and does not extend to trade secrets, intellectual property, or any other form of unwritten or unmarked data. For the purposes of this agreement, Confidential Information will not extend to marketing strategies, business plans, or financial forecasts, regardless of their labeling or sensitivity.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Confidential Information' is significantly narrower than the standard legal definition, excluding trade secrets and other forms of intellectual property unless explicitly labeled. This creates a conflict with how confidential information and trade secrets are legally protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ontario's Trade Secrets Act protects information that (a) is not generally known to the public and (b) has economic value, regardless of whether it's explicitly labeled 'confidential.' The modified contract language contradicts this by only protecting explicitly labeled information, potentially leaving trade secrets vulnerable.

perturbation.justification: The changed contract text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information,' excluding trade secrets and intellectual property unless explicitly labeled. This contradicts the general principle that trade secrets are protected regardless of explicit labeling, creating a misalignment with standard legal protections. The original text includes 'trade secrets, intellectual property' and other data whether 'designated as confidential' or 'disclosed in circumstances of confidence'. The changed text limits the definition to 'only information explicitly labeled as Confidential in writing'. Although the scraped snippet 1 is about trees, the Law Explanation states Ontario's Trade Secrets Act protects information that 'is not generally known to the public and (b) has economic value, regardless of whether it's explicitly labeled confidential.' Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction, since it does not reflect the law's protection of unlabeled trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Disclosure . All disclosures of Confidential Information (whether written or oral) by Party One and P a r t y T w o shall (a) remain in confidence for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure , except that any trade secrets or information with respect to Party One products or research and development will remain in confidence in perpetuity; (b) be disclosed only to employees of P a r t y T w o who have executed written confidentiality agreements wit h the employer; and (c) be reproduced or used by P a r t y T w o only to the extent necessary to fulfill it’s obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Disclosure . All disclosures of Confidential Information (whether written or oral) by Party One and P a r t y T w o shall (a) remain in confidence for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure, including trade secrets and research and development information; (b) be disclosed freely to any affiliate or subsidiary of P a r t y T w o, regardless of confidentiality agreements; and (c) be reproduced or used by P a r t y T w o for any purpose whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement protects trade secrets in perpetuity, restricts disclosure to employees with confidentiality agreements and limits use to fulfilling obligations. The modified version significantly reduces the confidentiality period, broadens disclosure permissions, and allows unrestricted use, weakening the protection of confidential information and trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a cause of action for the misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified contract language, by significantly limiting the protection period and allowing broad disclosure and use, weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets and could hinder a party's ability to pursue a claim under the DTSA if misappropriation occurs after the shortened confidentiality period or by an affiliate.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. perturbation.original_text: 13. Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Ontario and the laws of Canada applicable therein. Each party hereby attorn s to the jurisdiction of Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware. Each party hereby submits to the jurisdiction of Delaware, irrespective of the location of Confidential Information, the principal place of business of the parties, or the location of any breach.
perturbation.explanation: The original jurisdiction is Ontario. Changing it to Delaware introduces a legal conflict because neither party appears to have connections to Delaware, potentially making the choice of law and forum non-enforceable, especially considering it is an Ontario Canada K1Z 5M4 agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Private International Law (Choice of Law) Act, 2017, SO 2017, c 21 - Bill 59
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian conflict of laws rules generally require a real and substantial connection between the chosen jurisdiction (Delaware) and the agreement. The absence of such a connection could lead a Canadian court to disregard the Delaware choice of law provision and apply Ontario law instead, thus contradicting the contract's explicit term.
perturbation.justification: The change from Ontario to Delaware jurisdiction introduces a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states that neither party has connections to Delaware, and the Law Explanation supports this by stating that Canadian conflict of laws rules generally require a real and substantial connection between the chosen jurisdiction and the agreement. The absence of such a connection could lead a Canadian court to disregard the Delaware choice of law provision and apply Ontario law instead, thus contradicting the contract's explicit term. The absence of scraped law snippets does not diminish the impact of the 'real and substantial connection' rule being a critical component of jurisdictional enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Remedies . Party One and P a r t y T w o each acknowledge that compliance with the provisions of this Agreement is necessary to protec t their proprietary interes ts. Each party further acknowledges that any unauthorized use or disclosure to any person or entity in breach of this Agreement will result in irreparable and continuing damage, and that each party shall be authorized and entitled to obtain immediate injun ctive relief and any other rights or remedies to which it may be entitled. If either party violates any of the above agreements, it acknowledges that money damages will be an inadequate remedy and that the violated party will be entitled to specific perfo rmance or to injunctive relief to prohibit the violating party from continuing to violate this agreement even if no money damages can be proven.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Remedies . Party One and P a r t y T w o agree that in the event of a breach of this Agreement, the sole remedy shall be liquidated damages, capped at $100 CAD. Injunctive relief and specific performance are expressly waived by both parties, regardless of the nature or severity of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: The original allows for injunctive relief. The modified version limits the remedies to only liquidated damages and expressly waives injunctive relief. This contradicts the common law principle that injunctive relief is appropriate where monetary damages are inadequate, particularly for breaches of confidentiality where the harm is difficult to quantify.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Court of Justice Act, RSO 1990, c C.43, s 101
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 101 of the Ontario Court of Justice Act grants the Superior Court of Justice the power to grant injunctions. While parties can contractually limit remedies, a complete waiver of equitable remedies like injunctions, especially in cases involving confidential information, may be deemed unconscionable or against public policy if it effectively deprives a party of meaningful recourse for a breach that causes irreparable harm. The amended clause attempts to remove the court's jurisdiction from the act.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly waives injunctive relief, a remedy available under the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43. The original contract allows for injunctive relief, whereas the modified version limits remedies to liquidated damages, effectively removing a court's ability to grant an injunction. This contradicts the availability of remedies provided under law.
